---
title: "Troubleshoot TOU vs Seasonal Bill Delta Identity"
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

In `fig-quadrant-bar-natgas-tou-state` (line 3936 of `analysis.qmd`), the "Fair rate" and "TOU + fair rate (no flex)"
bars show identical quadrant percentages (14% / 72% / 13%). These bars come from different CAIRO runs:

- **Fair rate** (`natgas_switch_state`): `bill_after` from `bills_hprate_hp` = run **7+8** (seasonal rate, upgrade 2)
- **TOU + fair rate** (`natgas_tou_state`): `bill_after` from `bills_tou_hp` = run **11+12** (seasonal TOU rate, upgrade 2)

Both share the same `bill_before` (run 1+2, upgrade 0). If the runs produced different bills, the quadrant bars would
differ. This notebook walks through each layer of the computation to find where seasonal and TOU diverge (or fail to).

## Setup

```{python}
#| label: setup

from __future__ import annotations

import math

import polars as pl
from great_tables import GT
from lib.plotnine import SB_COLORS, theme_switchbox
from plotnine import (
    aes,
    annotate,
    coord_cartesian,
    geom_col,
    geom_histogram,
    geom_vline,
    ggplot,
    labs,
    scale_fill_manual,
    scale_y_continuous,
    theme,
)
```

## Parameters

Same parameters as `analysis.qmd`.

```{python}
#| label: params

BATCH = "ny_20260311b_r1-12_lmi_fix"

S3_BASE = "s3://data.sb/switchbox/cairo/outputs/hp_rates"
PATH_MASTER_BILLS_12 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_1+2/comb_bills_year_target/"
PATH_MASTER_BILLS_56 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_5+6/comb_bills_year_target/"
PATH_MASTER_BILLS_78 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_7+8/comb_bills_year_target/"
PATH_MASTER_BILLS_910 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_9+10/comb_bills_year_target/"
PATH_MASTER_BILLS_1112 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_11+12/comb_bills_year_target/"
PATH_METADATA_SB = "s3://data.sb/nrel/resstock/res_2024_amy2018_2_sb/metadata_utility/state=NY/utility_assignment.parquet"
```

## Constants

```{python}
#| label: constants

ANNUAL_MONTH = "Annual"
BLDG_ID = "bldg_id"
HEATING_TYPE_COL = "postprocess_group.heating_type"
COOLING_COL = "in.hvac_cooling_partial_space_conditioning"
REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG = 411608  # the "Buffalo home" used in analysis.qmd

UTIL_NAMES = {
    "cenhud": "Central Hudson",
    "coned": "Con Edison",
    "nimo": "National Grid",
    "nyseg": "NYSEG",
    "or": "O&R",
    "psegli": "PSEG-LI",
    "rge": "RG&E",
}
```

## Data ingest

We load the three master-bills datasets relevant to this investigation:

- **Run 1+2** — current rates, current HVAC (upgrade 0). This is the "before" for all scenarios.
- **Run 7+8** — seasonal HP rate, heat pump upgrade (upgrade 2). The "after" for the **fair rate** scenario.
- **Run 11+12** — TOU HP rate, heat pump upgrade (upgrade 2). The "after" for the **TOU + fair rate** scenario.

```{python}
#| label: ingest

bills_current = pl.scan_parquet(PATH_MASTER_BILLS_12, hive_partitioning=True)
bills_hprate_hp = pl.scan_parquet(PATH_MASTER_BILLS_78, hive_partitioning=True)
bills_tou_hp = pl.scan_parquet(PATH_MASTER_BILLS_1112, hive_partitioning=True)
```

Let's confirm the schemas:

```{python}
#| label: schemas

print("=== Run 1+2 schema ===")
print(bills_current.collect_schema())
print(f"\nRow count: {bills_current.select(pl.len()).collect().item()}")

print("\n=== Run 7+8 schema ===")
print(bills_hprate_hp.collect_schema())
print(f"\nRow count: {bills_hprate_hp.select(pl.len()).collect().item()}")

print("\n=== Run 11+12 schema ===")
print(bills_tou_hp.collect_schema())
print(f"\nRow count: {bills_tou_hp.select(pl.len()).collect().item()}")
```

## Step 1: Spot-check the S3 data

First, confirm that the paths point to distinct directories and have expected row counts.

```{python}
#| label: spot-check-paths

print("PATH_MASTER_BILLS_12:", PATH_MASTER_BILLS_12)
print("PATH_MASTER_BILLS_78:", PATH_MASTER_BILLS_78)
print("PATH_MASTER_BILLS_1112:", PATH_MASTER_BILLS_1112)
print()

for label, path in [("1+2", PATH_MASTER_BILLS_12), ("7+8", PATH_MASTER_BILLS_78), ("11+12", PATH_MASTER_BILLS_1112)]:
    lf = pl.scan_parquet(path, hive_partitioning=True)
    annual = lf.filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH)
    n_total = lf.select(pl.len()).collect().item()
    n_annual = annual.select(pl.len()).collect().item()
    n_utilities = annual.select(pl.col("sb.electric_utility").n_unique()).collect().item()
    print(f"Run {label}: {n_total:,} total rows, {n_annual:,} annual rows, {n_utilities} utilities")
```

## Step 2: Are the `energy_total_bill` values actually different?

We join the annual rows from runs 7+8 and 11+12 on `bldg_id` and compare `energy_total_bill`.

```{python}
#| label: compare-energy-total-bill

annual_78 = (
    bills_hprate_hp
    .filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH)
    .select(
        BLDG_ID,
        pl.col("energy_total_bill").alias("energy_total_78"),
        pl.col("elec_total_bill").alias("elec_total_78"),
        pl.col("elec_fixed_charge").alias("elec_fixed_78"),
        pl.col("elec_delivery_bill").alias("elec_delivery_78"),
        pl.col("elec_supply_bill").alias("elec_supply_78"),
        pl.col("gas_total_bill").alias("gas_total_78"),
        pl.col("oil_total_bill").alias("oil_total_78"),
        pl.col("propane_total_bill").alias("propane_total_78"),
        "heats_with_natgas",
        "sb.electric_utility",
    )
    .collect()
)

annual_1112 = (
    bills_tou_hp
    .filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH)
    .select(
        BLDG_ID,
        pl.col("energy_total_bill").alias("energy_total_1112"),
        pl.col("elec_total_bill").alias("elec_total_1112"),
        pl.col("elec_fixed_charge").alias("elec_fixed_1112"),
        pl.col("elec_delivery_bill").alias("elec_delivery_1112"),
        pl.col("elec_supply_bill").alias("elec_supply_1112"),
        pl.col("gas_total_bill").alias("gas_total_1112"),
        pl.col("oil_total_bill").alias("oil_total_1112"),
        pl.col("propane_total_bill").alias("propane_total_1112"),
    )
    .collect()
)

joined = annual_78.join(annual_1112, on=BLDG_ID, how="inner")
print(f"Joined rows: {len(joined):,}")
```

```{python}
#| label: energy-total-diff-summary

diff = joined.with_columns(
    (pl.col("energy_total_1112") - pl.col("energy_total_78")).alias("diff_energy_total"),
    (pl.col("elec_total_1112") - pl.col("elec_total_78")).alias("diff_elec_total"),
    (pl.col("elec_fixed_1112") - pl.col("elec_fixed_78")).alias("diff_elec_fixed"),
    (pl.col("elec_delivery_1112") - pl.col("elec_delivery_78")).alias("diff_elec_delivery"),
    (pl.col("elec_supply_1112") - pl.col("elec_supply_78")).alias("diff_elec_supply"),
    (pl.col("gas_total_1112") - pl.col("gas_total_78")).alias("diff_gas_total"),
    (pl.col("oil_total_1112") - pl.col("oil_total_78")).alias("diff_oil_total"),
    (pl.col("propane_total_1112") - pl.col("propane_total_78")).alias("diff_propane_total"),
)

# Overall: how many buildings have any difference in energy_total_bill?
n_differ = diff.filter(pl.col("diff_energy_total").abs() > 0.01).height
n_total = diff.height
print(f"Buildings with energy_total_bill difference > $0.01: {n_differ:,} / {n_total:,}")
print()

# Summary stats for the energy_total difference
for col in [c for c in diff.columns if c.startswith("diff_")]:
    s = diff[col]
    print(
        f"{col:30s}  "
        f"mean={s.mean():+.4f}  median={s.median():+.4f}  "
        f"min={s.min():+.4f}  max={s.max():+.4f}  "
        f"std={s.std():.4f}  "
        f"nonzero={diff.filter(pl.col(col).abs() > 0.01).height}"
    )
```

If all differences are zero, the problem is upstream: the master bills (or the CAIRO runs themselves) produced
identical output for runs 7+8 and 11+12.

```{python}
#| label: energy-total-diff-natgas-only

natgas_diff = diff.filter(pl.col("heats_with_natgas"))
n_ng_differ = natgas_diff.filter(pl.col("diff_energy_total").abs() > 0.01).height
print(f"Natgas buildings with energy_total difference: {n_ng_differ:,} / {natgas_diff.height:,}")

if n_ng_differ > 0:
    print("\nNatgas diff summary:")
    s = natgas_diff["diff_energy_total"]
    print(f"  mean={s.mean():+.2f}  median={s.median():+.2f}  min={s.min():+.2f}  max={s.max():+.2f}")
```

If the histogram below is a spike at zero, the TOU and seasonal runs produced identical bills and the
problem is upstream of the analysis (in the CAIRO runs or the master-bills build).

```{python}
#| label: histogram-energy-total-diff

(
    ggplot(natgas_diff, aes("diff_energy_total"))
    + geom_histogram(binwidth=5, fill=SB_COLORS["sky"], color="white")
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="red", linetype="dashed", size=1)
    + labs(
        title="Distribution of energy_total_bill difference (TOU − seasonal) for natgas buildings",
        x="TOU bill − seasonal bill ($/yr)",
        y="Building count",
    )
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(10.5, 4.5))
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-histogram-tou-delta-weighted

_weights = pl.read_parquet(PATH_METADATA_SB, columns=[BLDG_ID, "weight"])
_natgas_w = natgas_diff.join(_weights, on=BLDG_ID, how="left")

_BW = 10
_binned = (
    _natgas_w
    .with_columns(
        ((pl.col("diff_energy_total") / _BW).floor() * _BW + _BW / 2).alias("bin_center"),
    )
    .group_by("bin_center")
    .agg(pl.col("weight").sum().alias("weighted_count"))
    .filter((pl.col("bin_center") >= -200) & (pl.col("bin_center") <= 200))
    .sort("bin_center")
)

(
    ggplot(_binned, aes(x="bin_center", y="weighted_count"))
    + geom_col(width=_BW * 0.9, fill=SB_COLORS["sky"])
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="red", linetype="dashed", size=0.8)
    + coord_cartesian(xlim=(-200, 200))
    + scale_y_continuous(expand=(0, 0, 0.05, 0))
    + labs(
        title="Annual bill change, TOU − seasonal HP rate",
        x="Annual bill change ($/yr)",
        y="Number of homes",
    )
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(10.5, 4.5))
)
```

```{python}
#| label: tou-delta-report-vars
import pickle
from pathlib import Path

_total_w = _natgas_w["weight"].sum()
_within_50 = _natgas_w.filter(pl.col("diff_energy_total").abs() <= 50)["weight"].sum()

tou_delta_vars = {
    "pct_within_50": _within_50 / _total_w,
}

Path("../cache").mkdir(exist_ok=True)
Path("../cache/tou_delta_report_variables.pkl").write_bytes(pickle.dumps(tou_delta_vars))
```

## Step 3: Bill component breakdown — seasonal vs. TOU

For natgas buildings, we compare each bill component between runs 7+8 and 11+12 to isolate which components
are identical and which differ. We start with a global summary across all natgas buildings, then break it down
by utility to see whether any discrepancy is concentrated in a particular service territory.

```{python}
#| label: component-summary-overall

component_cols = [
    ("elec_fixed", "Electric Fixed Charge"),
    ("elec_delivery", "Electric Delivery (Volumetric)"),
    ("elec_supply", "Electric Supply"),
    ("gas_total", "Gas Total"),
    ("oil_total", "Oil Total"),
    ("propane_total", "Propane Total"),
    ("elec_total", "Electric Total"),
    ("energy_total", "Energy Total"),
]

# Overall per-component summary across all natgas buildings — not broken down by utility.
# A component with n_differ = 0 is identical across both runs.
records_overall = []
for short, label in component_cols:
    col = f"diff_{short}"
    s = natgas_diff[col]
    n_nonzero = natgas_diff.filter(pl.col(col).abs() > 0.01).height
    records_overall.append({
        "component": label,
        "n_differ": n_nonzero,
        "n_total": natgas_diff.height,
        "pct_differ": round(n_nonzero / natgas_diff.height * 100, 1),
        "mean_diff": round(float(s.mean()), 4),
        "median_diff": round(float(s.median()), 4),
        "max_abs_diff": round(float(s.abs().max()), 4),
    })

summary_overall = pl.DataFrame(records_overall)
print("Component comparison — all natgas buildings (TOU run 11+12 minus seasonal run 7+8):")
GT(summary_overall).fmt_number(columns=["mean_diff", "median_diff", "max_abs_diff"], decimals=4)
```

Components with `n_differ = 0` are bitwise-identical between the two runs. Any component that differs
isolates where TOU changes take effect (e.g., only `elec_delivery_bill` would differ if TOU only affects
the volumetric delivery piece). Now by utility:

```{python}
#| label: component-breakdown-by-utility

records = []
for util in sorted(natgas_diff["sb.electric_utility"].unique().to_list()):
    util_df = natgas_diff.filter(pl.col("sb.electric_utility") == util)
    for short, label in component_cols:
        col = f"diff_{short}"
        s = util_df[col]
        records.append({
            "utility": util,
            "component": label,
            "n_nonzero": util_df.filter(pl.col(col).abs() > 0.01).height,
            "n_bldgs": util_df.height,
            "mean_diff": round(float(s.mean()), 4),
            "max_abs_diff": round(float(s.abs().max()), 4),
        })

breakdown = pl.DataFrame(records)
GT(breakdown).fmt_number(columns=["mean_diff", "max_abs_diff"], decimals=4)
```

## Step 4: Representative building deep-dive

Show all bill components for building `411608` (the "Buffalo home") under all three scenarios side by side.

```{python}
#| label: representative-building

BILL_COLS = [
    BLDG_ID, "month",
    "elec_fixed_charge", "elec_delivery_bill", "elec_supply_bill", "elec_total_bill",
    "gas_total_bill", "oil_total_bill", "propane_total_bill", "energy_total_bill",
]

rep_12 = (
    bills_current
    .filter((pl.col(BLDG_ID) == REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG) & (pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH))
    .select(BILL_COLS)
    .collect()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("Run 1+2 (current, u0)").alias("scenario"))
)

rep_78 = (
    bills_hprate_hp
    .filter((pl.col(BLDG_ID) == REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG) & (pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH))
    .select(BILL_COLS)
    .collect()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("Run 7+8 (seasonal, u2)").alias("scenario"))
)

rep_1112 = (
    bills_tou_hp
    .filter((pl.col(BLDG_ID) == REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG) & (pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH))
    .select(BILL_COLS)
    .collect()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("Run 11+12 (TOU, u2)").alias("scenario"))
)

rep_all = pl.concat([rep_12, rep_78, rep_1112])

# Unpivot for a clean side-by-side comparison
value_cols = [c for c in BILL_COLS if c not in [BLDG_ID, "month"]]
rep_long = (
    rep_all
    .select(["scenario"] + value_cols)
    .unpivot(on=value_cols, index="scenario", variable_name="component", value_name="amount")
    .pivot(on="scenario", index="component", values="amount")
)

GT(rep_long).fmt_currency(
    columns=[c for c in rep_long.columns if c != "component"],
    decimals=2,
)
```

## Step 5: Delta distributions with quantile cutoffs

Reproduce the delta computation for both scenarios and compare their distributions for natgas buildings.

```{python}
#| label: compute-deltas

# Load metadata to get weight column (master bills may not include it)
metadata = pl.read_parquet(PATH_METADATA_SB, columns=[BLDG_ID, "weight"])

DELTA_SELECT = [
    BLDG_ID,
    pl.col("energy_total_bill").alias("bill_before"),
    "postprocess_group.has_hp",
    HEATING_TYPE_COL,
    "heats_with_natgas",
    "heats_with_oil",
    "heats_with_propane",
]

# Before: run 1+2 statewide
annual_current_state = (
    bills_current
    .filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH)
    .select(DELTA_SELECT)
    .collect()
    .join(metadata, on=BLDG_ID, how="left")
)

# After (seasonal): run 7+8
annual_after_switch_state = (
    bills_hprate_hp
    .filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH)
    .select(BLDG_ID, pl.col("energy_total_bill").alias("bill_after"))
    .collect()
)
delta_switch_state = annual_current_state.join(annual_after_switch_state, on=BLDG_ID, how="inner").with_columns(
    (pl.col("bill_after") - pl.col("bill_before")).alias("delta")
)

# After (TOU): run 11+12
annual_after_tou_state = (
    bills_tou_hp
    .filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH)
    .select(BLDG_ID, pl.col("energy_total_bill").alias("bill_after"))
    .collect()
)
delta_tou_state = annual_current_state.join(annual_after_tou_state, on=BLDG_ID, how="inner").with_columns(
    (pl.col("bill_after") - pl.col("bill_before")).alias("delta")
)

# Filter to natgas
natgas_switch = delta_switch_state.filter(pl.col("heats_with_natgas"))
natgas_tou = delta_tou_state.filter(pl.col("heats_with_natgas"))

print(f"Natgas buildings (seasonal): {natgas_switch.height:,}")
print(f"Natgas buildings (TOU):      {natgas_tou.height:,}")
```

```{python}
#| label: delta-distribution-comparison

def _weighted_percentile(df: pl.DataFrame, col: str, w_col: str, q: float) -> float:
    """Weighted percentile via cumulative weight."""
    s = df.sort(col)
    cum = s[w_col].cum_sum() / s[w_col].sum()
    return float(s.filter(cum >= q)[col][0])


quantiles = [0.01, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.99]
records = []
for q in quantiles:
    records.append({
        "quantile": f"p{int(q * 100):02d}",
        "seasonal_delta": round(_weighted_percentile(natgas_switch, "delta", "weight", q), 2),
        "tou_delta": round(_weighted_percentile(natgas_tou, "delta", "weight", q), 2),
    })

qtable = pl.DataFrame(records).with_columns(
    (pl.col("tou_delta") - pl.col("seasonal_delta")).alias("difference"),
)
GT(qtable).fmt_currency(columns=["seasonal_delta", "tou_delta", "difference"], decimals=2)
```

Representative building delta:

```{python}
#| label: representative-bldg-delta

rep_seasonal = natgas_switch.filter(pl.col(BLDG_ID) == REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG)
rep_tou = natgas_tou.filter(pl.col(BLDG_ID) == REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG)

if rep_seasonal.height > 0 and rep_tou.height > 0:
    print(f"Bldg {REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG} seasonal delta: ${rep_seasonal['delta'][0]:,.2f}")
    print(f"Bldg {REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG} TOU delta:      ${rep_tou['delta'][0]:,.2f}")
    print(f"Difference:                   ${rep_tou['delta'][0] - rep_seasonal['delta'][0]:,.2f}")
else:
    print(f"Bldg {REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG} not found in natgas subset")
```

Now we visualize the two delta distributions overlaid on a single histogram. Dashed vertical lines mark the
1st, 25th, 50th, 75th, and 99th weighted percentiles for each scenario (sky = seasonal, carrot = TOU). A
text annotation marks where the representative building falls. If the two distributions are identical, the
bars and vlines will stack exactly.

```{python}
#| label: delta-overlaid-histogram

_BIN_WIDTH = 100  # $100 bins


def _bin_for_overlay(df: pl.DataFrame, scenario: str, bin_width: int = _BIN_WIDTH) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Bin delta values into a weighted histogram DataFrame tagged with *scenario*."""
    return (
        df.select("delta", "weight")
        .with_columns(
            ((pl.col("delta") / bin_width).floor() * bin_width + bin_width / 2).alias("bin_center"),
        )
        .group_by("bin_center")
        .agg(pl.col("weight").sum().alias("weight_sum"))
        .with_columns(pl.lit(scenario).alias("scenario"))
    )


_seasonal_bins = _bin_for_overlay(natgas_switch, "Seasonal (run 7+8)")
_tou_bins = _bin_for_overlay(natgas_tou, "TOU (run 11+12)")
_all_bins = pl.concat([_seasonal_bins, _tou_bins])

# Axis limits from 1st/99th percentile of the combined distribution so both tails are visible.
_combined_deltas = pl.concat([
    natgas_switch.select("delta", "weight"),
    natgas_tou.select("delta", "weight"),
])
_snap = max(_BIN_WIDTH, 50)
_x_lo = math.floor(_weighted_percentile(_combined_deltas, "delta", "weight", 0.01) / _snap) * _snap
_x_hi = math.ceil(_weighted_percentile(_combined_deltas, "delta", "weight", 0.99) / _snap) * _snap

# Weighted quantiles (p01, p25, p50, p75, p99) for each scenario.
_q_levels = [0.01, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.99]
_q_labels = ["p01", "p25", "p50", "p75", "p99"]
_seasonal_qs = {lbl: _weighted_percentile(natgas_switch, "delta", "weight", q)
                for lbl, q in zip(_q_labels, _q_levels)}
_tou_qs = {lbl: _weighted_percentile(natgas_tou, "delta", "weight", q)
           for lbl, q in zip(_q_labels, _q_levels)}

print("Seasonal quantiles:", {k: f"${v:,.0f}" for k, v in _seasonal_qs.items()})
print("TOU quantiles:     ", {k: f"${v:,.0f}" for k, v in _tou_qs.items()})

# Representative building's delta in each scenario.
_rep_row_s = natgas_switch.filter(pl.col(BLDG_ID) == REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG)
_rep_row_t = natgas_tou.filter(pl.col(BLDG_ID) == REPRESENTATIVE_BLDG)
_rep_delta_s = float(_rep_row_s["delta"][0]) if _rep_row_s.height > 0 else None
_rep_delta_t = float(_rep_row_t["delta"][0]) if _rep_row_t.height > 0 else None

# Max bar height (within axis window) for annotation y-placement.
_max_w = float(
    _all_bins
    .filter((pl.col("bin_center") >= _x_lo) & (pl.col("bin_center") <= _x_hi))
    ["weight_sum"].max()
)
_y_annot_top = _max_w * 1.15
_y_annot_mid = _max_w * 0.85

_scenario_colors = {
    "Seasonal (run 7+8)": SB_COLORS["sky"],
    "TOU (run 11+12)": SB_COLORS["carrot"],
}
_scenario_dtype = pl.Enum(["Seasonal (run 7+8)", "TOU (run 11+12)"])
_plot_df = _all_bins.with_columns(pl.col("scenario").cast(_scenario_dtype))

p = (
    ggplot(_plot_df, aes(x="bin_center", y="weight_sum", fill="scenario"))
    + geom_col(width=_BIN_WIDTH * 0.85, position="identity", alpha=0.55)
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, linetype="solid", color="#888888", size=0.8)
    + scale_fill_manual(values=_scenario_colors)
    + coord_cartesian(xlim=(_x_lo, _x_hi))
    + labs(
        title="Delta distribution: seasonal vs. TOU — natgas buildings (weighted)",
        x="Annual Bill Change ($/yr)",
        y="Weighted # of Homes",
        fill="Scenario",
    )
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(10.5, 5.0))
)

# Seasonal quantile vlines — dashed, sky blue.
for _lbl, _val in _seasonal_qs.items():
    if _x_lo <= _val <= _x_hi:
        p = p + geom_vline(xintercept=_val, linetype="dashed", color=SB_COLORS["sky"], size=0.7, alpha=0.9)

# TOU quantile vlines — dotted, carrot.
for _lbl, _val in _tou_qs.items():
    if _x_lo <= _val <= _x_hi:
        p = p + geom_vline(xintercept=_val, linetype="dotted", color=SB_COLORS["carrot"], size=0.7, alpha=0.9)

# Representative building annotation(s).
if _rep_delta_s is not None and _rep_delta_t is not None:
    _same = abs(_rep_delta_s - _rep_delta_t) < 0.01
    if _same:
        # Identical — one combined label.
        p = p + annotate(
            "text", x=_rep_delta_s, y=_y_annot_top,
            label=f"Rep bldg (both)\n${_rep_delta_s:,.0f}",
            ha="center", va="top", size=9, color="#333333",
        )
    else:
        p = p + annotate(
            "text", x=_rep_delta_s, y=_y_annot_top,
            label=f"Rep bldg\n(seasonal)\n${_rep_delta_s:,.0f}",
            ha="center", va="top", size=9, color=SB_COLORS["sky"],
        )
        p = p + annotate(
            "text", x=_rep_delta_t, y=_y_annot_mid,
            label=f"Rep bldg\n(TOU)\n${_rep_delta_t:,.0f}",
            ha="center", va="top", size=9, color=SB_COLORS["carrot"],
        )

p
```

## Step 6: Quadrant percentages table

Print the exact quadrant percentages for all three scenarios side by side so rounding can be verified and any
differences smaller than 1% (which would appear identical in the chart labels) can be spotted.

```{python}
#| label: quadrant-pcts-helper

def quadrant_pcts(df: pl.DataFrame) -> dict[str, float]:
    """Weighted percentage of homes in each bill-change quadrant."""
    total = df["weight"].sum()
    return {
        "savings > $1k":  df.filter(pl.col("delta") < -1000)["weight"].sum() / total * 100,
        "savings $0-1k":  df.filter((pl.col("delta") >= -1000) & (pl.col("delta") < 0))["weight"].sum() / total * 100,
        "losses $0-1k":   df.filter((pl.col("delta") >= 0) & (pl.col("delta") < 1000))["weight"].sum() / total * 100,
        "losses > $1k":   df.filter(pl.col("delta") >= 1000)["weight"].sum() / total * 100,
    }
```

For the default-rate bar we need run 3+4 (HP on flat rate), which isn't loaded above. We load it here.

```{python}
#| label: quadrant-table

PATH_MASTER_BILLS_34 = f"{S3_BASE}/ny/all_utilities/{BATCH}/run_3+4/comb_bills_year_target/"
bills_hp = pl.scan_parquet(PATH_MASTER_BILLS_34, hive_partitioning=True)

annual_hp_state = (
    bills_hp
    .filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH)
    .select(BLDG_ID, pl.col("energy_total_bill").alias("bill_after"))
    .collect()
)
delta_default_state = (
    annual_current_state
    .join(annual_hp_state, on=BLDG_ID, how="inner")
    .with_columns((pl.col("bill_after") - pl.col("bill_before")).alias("delta"))
)
natgas_default = delta_default_state.filter(pl.col("heats_with_natgas")).select("delta", "weight")

pct_default   = quadrant_pcts(natgas_default)
pct_seasonal  = quadrant_pcts(natgas_switch.select("delta", "weight"))
pct_tou       = quadrant_pcts(natgas_tou.select("delta", "weight"))

records = []
for q in ["savings > $1k", "savings $0-1k", "losses $0-1k", "losses > $1k"]:
    records.append({
        "quadrant": q,
        "default_rate (run 3+4)":    round(pct_default[q],  4),
        "fair_rate (run 7+8)":       round(pct_seasonal[q], 4),
        "tou_fair (run 11+12)":      round(pct_tou[q],      4),
        "seasonal − tou":            round(pct_seasonal[q] - pct_tou[q], 4),
    })

GT(pl.DataFrame(records)).fmt_number(
    columns=["default_rate (run 3+4)", "fair_rate (run 7+8)", "tou_fair (run 11+12)", "seasonal − tou"],
    decimals=4,
)
```

If `seasonal − tou` is zero for all quadrants, the two runs produced bitwise-identical `energy_total_bill` values
and the problem lies upstream (either the master-bills build used the same source data for both, or CAIRO run 11
produced the same output as run 7 due to a configuration issue).

## Step 7: Building characteristic drivers

The histogram in Step 2 showed that TOU's effect vs. seasonal has a spread of roughly ±$300/yr across natgas
buildings. Here we investigate *which* building characteristics predict whether TOU helps or hurts a customer.

TOU peak hours cluster in the late afternoon/early evening (3–8 PM). Summer peak/off-peak ratios (3–4×) are
much higher than winter (1.6–2×). Buildings that consume more electricity during those summer afternoon
peak windows pay more under TOU; buildings that are heating-dominated and use electricity mostly off-peak
save under TOU.

### 7a — Load ResStock metadata

All metadata needed for this analysis — floor area (`in.sqft`), building type
(`in.geometry_building_type_recs`), climate zone (`in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004`), vintage
(`in.vintage_acs`), county (`in.county`), and cooling presence (`in.hvac_cooling_partial_space_conditioning`)
— are available directly from the utility-assignment parquet (`PATH_METADATA_SB`). We join them to
`natgas_diff` on `bldg_id`.

```{python}
#| label: load-resstock-metadata

_META_COLS = [
    BLDG_ID,
    "in.sqft",
    "in.geometry_building_type_recs",
    "in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004",
    "in.vintage_acs",
    "in.county",
    COOLING_COL,
]

metadata_raw = pl.read_parquet(PATH_METADATA_SB, columns=_META_COLS)
print(f"Loaded metadata: {metadata_raw.shape[0]:,} buildings × {len(_META_COLS)} columns")

# natgas_diff (from Step 2) has bill-diff columns but no weight.  Attach weights
# from natgas_switch (Step 5) so that utility-level summaries in 7b can be weighted.
natgas_meta = (
    natgas_diff
    .join(metadata_raw, on=BLDG_ID, how="left")
    .join(natgas_switch.select(BLDG_ID, "weight"), on=BLDG_ID, how="left")
)
n_matched = natgas_meta.filter(pl.col("in.sqft").is_not_null()).height
print(f"natgas buildings matched to metadata: {n_matched:,} / {natgas_meta.height:,}")
```

Let's confirm the enriched table looks right:

```{python}
#| label: metadata-preview

natgas_meta.select([
    BLDG_ID,
    "sb.electric_utility",
    "diff_energy_total",
    "in.sqft",
    "in.geometry_building_type_recs",
    "in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004",
    "in.vintage_acs",
    COOLING_COL,
]).head(5)
```

### 7b — Utility-level summary with rate context

Before diving into building-level drivers (Step 7c onward), we establish the utility-level baseline:
which service territories show the largest TOU vs. seasonal spread, and does the TOU window design
explain it?

The table below shows, for each utility:

- **Weighted mean delta** (TOU − seasonal), in $/yr — positive means TOU is more expensive on average
- **% saving under TOU** (weighted share of buildings where TOU is cheaper)
- **Peak window hours** (N) and start–end range for winter and summer (from the March 2026 HP-only
  sweep documented in `context/tools/tou_window_optimization.md`)
- **Peak/off-peak ratio** for winter and summer — higher ratios create larger TOU spread

```{python}
#| label: tou-rate-specs

# TOU window specs from context/tools/tou_window_optimization.md (NY HP-only sweep, March 2026).
# Peak hours are 0-indexed (hour 15 = 3 PM, hour 20 = 8 PM).
_TOU_SPECS = pl.DataFrame({
    "utility": ["cenhud", "coned", "nimo", "nyseg", "or", "psegli", "rge"],
    "peak_hours_n": [5, 3, 3, 3, 5, 5, 3],
    "winter_peak_window": ["16–20", "16–18", "17–19", "17–19", "16–20", "16–20", "17–19"],
    "summer_peak_window": ["15–19", "15–17", "17–19", "17–19", "15–19", "15–19", "17–19"],
    "winter_ratio": [1.57, 1.99, 1.75, 1.75, 1.57, 1.67, 1.75],
    "summer_ratio": [3.04, 4.28, 2.91, 2.93, 3.12, 3.80, 2.87],
})
```

```{python}
#| label: utility-summary-table

_util_labels = pl.DataFrame({
    "utility": list(UTIL_NAMES.keys()),
    "utility_label": list(UTIL_NAMES.values()),
})

_util_summary = (
    natgas_meta
    .group_by("sb.electric_utility")
    .agg(
        pl.len().alias("n_bldgs"),
        pl.col("weight").sum().alias("n_weighted"),
        (
            (pl.col("diff_energy_total") * pl.col("weight")).sum()
            / pl.col("weight").sum()
        ).alias("mean_delta_wtd"),
        (
            pl.when(pl.col("diff_energy_total") < 0)
            .then(pl.col("weight"))
            .otherwise(0)
            .sum()
            / pl.col("weight").sum()
            * 100
        ).alias("pct_saving_wtd"),
    )
    .rename({"sb.electric_utility": "utility"})
    .join(_TOU_SPECS, on="utility", how="left")
    .join(_util_labels, on="utility", how="left")
    .sort("mean_delta_wtd")
    .select([
        "utility_label",
        "n_bldgs",
        "mean_delta_wtd",
        "pct_saving_wtd",
        "peak_hours_n",
        "winter_peak_window",
        "summer_peak_window",
        "winter_ratio",
        "summer_ratio",
    ])
)

(
    GT(_util_summary)
    .tab_header(
        title="TOU vs. Seasonal — utility-level summary with rate design context",
        subtitle="Natgas buildings; weighted statistics; TOU specs from HP-only window sweep (March 2026)",
    )
    .cols_label(
        utility_label="Utility",
        n_bldgs="# Bldgs",
        mean_delta_wtd="Wtd mean Δ ($/yr)",
        pct_saving_wtd="% saving (wtd)",
        peak_hours_n="N peak hrs",
        winter_peak_window="Winter window",
        summer_peak_window="Summer window",
        winter_ratio="Winter ratio",
        summer_ratio="Summer ratio",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=["mean_delta_wtd"], decimals=2)
    .fmt_number(columns=["pct_saving_wtd", "winter_ratio", "summer_ratio"], decimals=2)
    .fmt_integer(columns=["n_bldgs", "peak_hours_n"])
)
```

### 7c — Segmented box plots

For each of six building-characteristic dimensions, we produce a horizontal box plot of `diff_energy_total`
(TOU − seasonal). Box statistics are **unweighted** (each sampled building has equal weight in the IQR
calculation); weighted building counts are annotated at the right edge of each row. A red dashed zero-line
separates buildings that save under TOU (negative delta) from those that pay more. Groups are sorted by
weighted mean delta, so the TOU-friendliest segment appears at the top.

```{python}
#| label: boxplot-imports

from plotnine import (
    coord_flip,
    geom_boxplot,
    geom_hline,
    geom_point,
    geom_text,
    scale_color_manual,
)
```

We attach CAIRO sample weights (from `natgas_switch`, which carries them from `annual_current_state`) and
derive a floor-area bin column. We use `drop("weight", strict=False)` first so the join is safe whether or
not `natgas_meta` already has a `weight` column.

```{python}
#| label: enrich-natgas-meta

SQFT_ORDER = ["<1,000 sq ft", "1,000–2,000 sq ft", "2,000–3,000 sq ft", ">3,000 sq ft"]

natgas_meta_w = (
    natgas_meta.drop("weight", strict=False)
    .join(natgas_switch.select(BLDG_ID, "weight"), on=BLDG_ID, how="left")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("in.sqft") < 1000)
        .then(pl.lit("<1,000 sq ft"))
        .when(pl.col("in.sqft") < 2000)
        .then(pl.lit("1,000–2,000 sq ft"))
        .when(pl.col("in.sqft") < 3000)
        .then(pl.lit("2,000–3,000 sq ft"))
        .otherwise(pl.lit(">3,000 sq ft"))
        .alias("sqft_bin")
    )
)

n_w = natgas_meta_w.filter(pl.col("weight").is_not_null()).height
print(f"natgas_meta_w: {natgas_meta_w.height:,} buildings, {n_w:,} with weights")
```

The helper function below is reused for all six dimensions. Groups are sorted by weighted mean delta by
default; pass an explicit `order` list to override (e.g., for a natural size or time ordering).

```{python}
#| label: boxplot-helper


def make_segment_boxplot(
    df: pl.DataFrame,
    group_col: str,
    x_label: str,
    title: str,
    order: list[str] | None = None,
    label_map: dict[str, str] | None = None,
    fig_height: float | None = None,
) -> ggplot:
    """Horizontal box plot of diff_energy_total grouped by group_col.

    Parameters
    ----------
    df:
        DataFrame with columns ``group_col``, ``diff_energy_total``, and ``weight``.
    group_col:
        Column name to group by (original name in *df*).
    x_label:
        Axis label for the category dimension (y-axis label after coord_flip).
    title:
        Plot title.
    order:
        Category order (values in *df* before label mapping), bottom-to-top after
        coord_flip.  When ``None`` groups are sorted ascending by weighted mean delta
        (TOU-friendliest at top).
    label_map:
        Optional mapping from raw group values to display labels.
    fig_height:
        Figure height in inches; defaults to ``max(3.5, n_groups * 0.7 + 1.5)``.
    """
    plot_df = (
        df.select([group_col, "diff_energy_total", "weight"])
        .rename({group_col: "group"})
        .drop_nulls(subset=["group", "diff_energy_total"])
    )
    if label_map:
        plot_df = plot_df.with_columns(pl.col("group").replace(label_map))

    # Determine display order
    if order is None:
        order_vals = (
            plot_df.group_by("group")
            .agg(
                (
                    (pl.col("diff_energy_total") * pl.col("weight")).sum()
                    / pl.col("weight").sum()
                ).alias("wtd_mean")
            )
            .sort("wtd_mean")
            ["group"]
            .to_list()
        )
    else:
        order_vals = [label_map.get(o, o) for o in order] if label_map else list(order)

    plot_df = plot_df.with_columns(pl.col("group").cast(pl.Enum(order_vals)))

    # Weighted counts per group for annotations
    wt_counts = (
        plot_df.group_by("group")
        .agg(pl.col("weight").sum().round(0).cast(pl.Int64).alias("wtd_n"))
        .with_columns(
            pl.col("wtd_n")
            .map_elements(lambda n: f"n={n:,}", return_dtype=pl.String)
            .alias("label"),
            pl.col("group").cast(pl.Enum(order_vals)),
        )
    )

    # Axis limits (1st–99th percentile of the raw distribution, with headroom for labels)
    p01 = float(df["diff_energy_total"].drop_nulls().quantile(0.01))
    p99 = float(df["diff_energy_total"].drop_nulls().quantile(0.99))
    span = abs(p99 - p01)
    y_hi = p99 + span * 0.22  # headroom for count labels
    wt_counts = wt_counts.with_columns(pl.lit(y_hi * 0.97).alias("y_pos"))

    n_groups = plot_df["group"].n_unique()
    height = fig_height or max(3.5, n_groups * 0.7 + 1.5)

    return (
        ggplot(plot_df, aes(x="group", y="diff_energy_total"))
        + geom_boxplot(
            fill=SB_COLORS["sky"],
            alpha=0.65,
            outlier_size=0.4,
            outlier_alpha=0.2,
        )
        + geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype="dashed", color="#cc3333", size=0.8)
        + geom_text(
            wt_counts,
            aes(x="group", y="y_pos", label="label"),
            ha="right",
            size=8,
            color="#555555",
        )
        + coord_flip(ylim=(p01, y_hi))
        + labs(title=title, x=x_label, y="TOU − Seasonal ($/yr)")
        + theme_switchbox()
        + theme(figure_size=(10.5, height))
    )
```

#### Utility

ConEd's 3-hour peak window with its 4.3× summer ratio produces the largest per-building TOU swings; CenHud
and O&R's 5-hour windows with softer ratios should show a different profile.

```{python}
#| label: fig-boxplot-utility
#| fig-cap: "TOU vs. seasonal annual bill delta by electric utility (natgas HP buildings, sorted by weighted mean delta)"

make_segment_boxplot(
    natgas_meta_w,
    "sb.electric_utility",
    x_label="Utility",
    title="TOU vs. seasonal delta by utility",
    label_map=UTIL_NAMES,
)
```

#### Building type

Multi-family buildings have less total heating/cooling load per unit, which may compress the TOU impact;
large single-family detached homes have more.

```{python}
#| label: fig-boxplot-bldg-type
#| fig-cap: "TOU vs. seasonal annual bill delta by building type (natgas HP buildings, sorted by weighted mean delta)"

make_segment_boxplot(
    natgas_meta_w,
    "in.geometry_building_type_recs",
    x_label="Building type",
    title="TOU vs. seasonal delta by building type",
)
```

#### Climate zone

Zone 4A (NYC metro area) has more summer cooling load concentrated in the afternoon peak window; zones 5A
and 6A (upstate) are more heating-dominated with less summer AC exposure.

```{python}
#| label: fig-boxplot-climate-zone
#| fig-cap: "TOU vs. seasonal annual bill delta by ASHRAE climate zone (natgas HP buildings, sorted by weighted mean delta)"

make_segment_boxplot(
    natgas_meta_w,
    "in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004",
    x_label="ASHRAE climate zone",
    title="TOU vs. seasonal delta by climate zone",
    fig_height=4.5,
)
```

#### Cooling presence

Buildings with central AC or room AC run compressors heavily during summer afternoon peak hours (3–8 PM) —
exactly the TOU peak window. Buildings with no cooling have only their HP heating load, which is mostly
off-peak overnight.

```{python}
#| label: fig-boxplot-cooling
#| fig-cap: "TOU vs. seasonal annual bill delta by cooling system presence (natgas HP buildings, sorted by weighted mean delta)"

make_segment_boxplot(
    natgas_meta_w,
    COOLING_COL,
    x_label="Cooling system",
    title="TOU vs. seasonal delta by cooling presence",
)
```

#### Floor area

Larger homes have more total heating and cooling load, which amplifies the TOU impact in either direction.
A <1,000 sq ft apartment and a >3,000 sq ft suburban home both face the same rate structure, but the larger
home's bigger electricity bill means more dollars at stake when peak/off-peak pricing applies.

```{python}
#| label: fig-boxplot-sqft
#| fig-cap: "TOU vs. seasonal annual bill delta by floor area bin (natgas HP buildings, ordered small to large)"

make_segment_boxplot(
    natgas_meta_w,
    "sqft_bin",
    x_label="Floor area",
    title="TOU vs. seasonal delta by floor area",
    order=SQFT_ORDER,
    fig_height=4.5,
)
```

#### Vintage

Older buildings tend to have worse envelope insulation, leading to higher heating and cooling loads per
square foot. More load means a larger absolute TOU impact (positive or negative); the direction depends on
whether cooling or heating dominates.

```{python}
#| label: fig-boxplot-vintage
#| fig-cap: "TOU vs. seasonal annual bill delta by building vintage (natgas HP buildings, sorted by weighted mean delta)"

_vin_order = sorted(natgas_meta_w["in.vintage_acs"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list())

make_segment_boxplot(
    natgas_meta_w,
    "in.vintage_acs",
    x_label="Building vintage",
    title="TOU vs. seasonal delta by vintage",
    order=_vin_order,
)
```

### 7d — Electricity consumption scatter

Higher annual electricity use amplifies the absolute TOU impact — but whether TOU helps or hurts depends on
*when* that electricity is consumed. Buildings in summer-cooling-heavy climate zone 4A (NYC metro) are
expected to cluster in the upper half (positive delta: TOU is more expensive); heating-dominated zones 5A
and 6A (upstate) should appear in the lower half (negative delta: TOU saves money). Within each zone, the
horizontal spread reflects load volume: buildings with higher electric delivery bills have more kWh at stake.

`elec_delivery_78` (electric delivery bill under the seasonal HP rate, run 7+8) is a proxy for total annual
HP electricity consumption since delivery charges are volumetric.

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter-elec-tou
#| fig-cap: "Annual electric delivery bill (seasonal HP rate) vs. TOU–seasonal annual bill delta, colored by ASHRAE climate zone (natgas HP buildings)"

_CZ_COLORS = {
    "4A": SB_COLORS["carrot"],
    "5A": SB_COLORS["sky"],
    "6A": SB_COLORS["pistachio"],
}

_scatter_df = (
    natgas_meta_w
    .select(["elec_delivery_78", "diff_energy_total", "in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004"])
    .drop_nulls()
    .rename({"in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004": "climate_zone"})
)

_cz_vals = sorted(_scatter_df["climate_zone"].unique().to_list())
_cz_color_vals = {cz: _CZ_COLORS.get(cz, SB_COLORS["midnight"]) for cz in _cz_vals}

(
    ggplot(_scatter_df, aes(x="elec_delivery_78", y="diff_energy_total", color="climate_zone"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.15, size=0.7)
    + geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype="dashed", color="#cc3333", size=0.8)
    + scale_color_manual(values=_cz_color_vals)
    + labs(
        title="Electricity use vs. TOU–seasonal delta, by climate zone",
        x="Annual electric delivery bill — seasonal HP rate ($/yr)",
        y="TOU − Seasonal ($/yr)",
        color="Climate zone",
    )
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(10.5, 5.5))
)
```

### 7e — Winners/losers summary table

A single consolidated table spanning all six dimensions. For each segment we show the weighted-mean delta,
weighted-median delta, the share of buildings that save under TOU, and the weighted customer count.
Rows within each dimension are sorted by weighted-mean delta ascending so the most TOU-friendly segments
appear first.

```{python}
#| label: winners-losers-helper


def _segment_stats(
    df: pl.DataFrame,
    col: str,
    dimension_label: str,
    label_map: dict[str, str] | None = None,
) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Compute weighted summary stats for diff_energy_total grouped by *col*.

    Returns one row per unique non-null value in *col* with columns:
    dimension, segment, wtd_mean, wtd_median, pct_saving, wtd_n.
    """
    s = (
        df.select([col, "diff_energy_total", "weight"])
        .drop_nulls(subset=[col, "diff_energy_total", "weight"])
    )
    if label_map:
        s = s.with_columns(pl.col(col).replace(label_map))

    rows: list[dict] = []
    for grp_val in s[col].unique().to_list():
        g = s.filter(pl.col(col) == grp_val)
        total_w = float(g["weight"].sum())
        wtd_mean = float((g["diff_energy_total"] * g["weight"]).sum() / total_w)
        g_sorted = g.sort("diff_energy_total")
        cum_w = g_sorted["weight"].cum_sum() / total_w
        wtd_med = float(g_sorted.filter(cum_w >= 0.5)["diff_energy_total"][0])
        pct_save = float(
            g.filter(pl.col("diff_energy_total") < 0)["weight"].sum() / total_w * 100
        )
        rows.append({
            "dimension": dimension_label,
            "segment": str(grp_val),
            "wtd_mean": round(wtd_mean, 2),
            "wtd_median": round(wtd_med, 2),
            "pct_saving": round(pct_save, 1),
            "wtd_n": int(round(total_w)),
        })
    return pl.DataFrame(rows).sort("wtd_mean")
```

```{python}
#| label: winners-losers-table

_DIMS: list[tuple[str, str, dict[str, str] | None]] = [
    ("sb.electric_utility",             "Utility",        UTIL_NAMES),
    ("in.geometry_building_type_recs",  "Building type",  None),
    ("in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004","Climate zone",   None),
    (COOLING_COL,                       "Cooling",        None),
    ("sqft_bin",                        "Floor area",     None),
    ("in.vintage_acs",                  "Vintage",        None),
]

_all_stats = pl.concat([
    _segment_stats(natgas_meta_w, col, dim, lmap)
    for col, dim, lmap in _DIMS
])

# Sort: by dimension (preserving _DIMS order), then by wtd_mean ascending within each.
_dim_order = [d for _, d, _ in _DIMS]
_all_stats = (
    _all_stats
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("dimension")
        .cast(pl.Enum(_dim_order))
        .alias("_dim_sort")
    )
    .sort(["_dim_sort", "wtd_mean"])
    .drop("_dim_sort")
)

(
    GT(_all_stats)
    .tab_header(
        title="Who saves under TOU vs. seasonal? Winners and losers by building characteristic",
        subtitle=(
            "Natgas HP buildings; weighted statistics. "
            "Positive Δ = TOU more expensive than seasonal. "
            "Sorted by weighted mean delta within each dimension (most TOU-friendly first)."
        ),
    )
    .cols_label(
        dimension="Dimension",
        segment="Segment",
        wtd_mean="Wtd mean Δ ($/yr)",
        wtd_median="Wtd median Δ ($/yr)",
        pct_saving="% saving (wtd)",
        wtd_n="Wtd customers",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=["wtd_mean", "wtd_median"], decimals=2)
    .fmt_number(columns=["pct_saving"], decimals=1)
    .fmt_integer(columns=["wtd_n"])
    .tab_source_note(
        "Δ = TOU (run 11+12) minus seasonal (run 7+8) annual energy bill. "
        "% saving = share of buildings (by weight) where TOU is cheaper."
    )
)
```

## Step 8: In-sample HP household analysis (upgrade 0)

All preceding steps compared runs 7+8 (seasonal, upgrade 2) and 11+12 (TOU, upgrade 2), which are
**out-of-sample**: the loads come from buildings that have already converted to heat pumps. The TOU rate
windows and peak/off-peak ratios, however, were **fit** to the **in-sample** population: HP customers in
upgrade 0 (current HVAC, but with existing HPs in the baseline stock). That fitting happened in:

- **Run 5+6** — HP seasonal rate, upgrade 0 precalc
- **Run 9+10** — HP TOU rate, upgrade 0 precalc

This section verifies that TOU is indeed better than seasonal for those in-sample HP households in all
utilities, and characterizes who those households are.

### 8a — Load in-sample bill data

```{python}
#| label: ingest-insample

bills_default_u0 = pl.scan_parquet(PATH_MASTER_BILLS_12, hive_partitioning=True)
bills_seasonal_u0 = pl.scan_parquet(PATH_MASTER_BILLS_56, hive_partitioning=True)
bills_tou_u0 = pl.scan_parquet(PATH_MASTER_BILLS_910, hive_partitioning=True)

for label, path in [
    ("run 1+2 (default, u0)",  PATH_MASTER_BILLS_12),
    ("run 5+6 (seasonal, u0)", PATH_MASTER_BILLS_56),
    ("run 9+10 (TOU, u0)",     PATH_MASTER_BILLS_910),
]:
    lf = pl.scan_parquet(path, hive_partitioning=True)
    n = lf.filter(pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH).select(pl.len()).collect().item()
    print(f"{label}: {n:,} annual rows")
```

### 8b — Filter to in-sample HP households

The in-sample HP population is defined as buildings with `postprocess_group.has_hp == True` in upgrade 0.
These are buildings that already had heat pumps in the ResStock baseline (before any retrofit). CAIRO fit
the TOU windows and peak/off-peak ratios to minimize cross-subsidization for exactly this group.

```{python}
#| label: filter-insample-hp

# Metadata carries has_hp, weight, and ResStock building characteristics.
_META_INSAMPLE_COLS = [
    BLDG_ID,
    "weight",
    "postprocess_group.has_hp",
    HEATING_TYPE_COL,
    "in.sqft",
    "in.geometry_building_type_recs",
    "in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004",
    "in.vintage_acs",
    COOLING_COL,
    "sb.electric_utility",
]
metadata_insample = pl.read_parquet(PATH_METADATA_SB, columns=_META_INSAMPLE_COLS)

hp_bldg_ids = (
    metadata_insample
    .filter(pl.col("postprocess_group.has_hp"))
    [BLDG_ID]
    .to_list()
)
print(f"In-sample HP buildings (upgrade 0): {len(hp_bldg_ids):,}")
print(
    f"Weighted HP customer count: "
    f"{metadata_insample.filter(pl.col('postprocess_group.has_hp'))['weight'].sum():,.0f}"
)

# Annual bills for the three runs, filtered to HP buildings only.
def _annual_hp(lf: pl.LazyFrame, bill_alias: str) -> pl.DataFrame:
    return (
        lf
        .filter(
            (pl.col("month") == ANNUAL_MONTH) &
            (pl.col(BLDG_ID).is_in(hp_bldg_ids))
        )
        .select(BLDG_ID, "sb.electric_utility", pl.col("energy_total_bill").alias(bill_alias))
        .collect()
    )


annual_default_hp  = _annual_hp(bills_default_u0,  "bill_default")
annual_seasonal_hp = _annual_hp(bills_seasonal_u0, "bill_seasonal")
annual_tou_hp      = _annual_hp(bills_tou_u0,      "bill_tou")

print(f"\nAnnual HP rows — default:  {annual_default_hp.height:,}")
print(f"Annual HP rows — seasonal: {annual_seasonal_hp.height:,}")
print(f"Annual HP rows — TOU:      {annual_tou_hp.height:,}")
```

### 8c — Compute bill deltas

```{python}
#| label: compute-insample-deltas

# Join all three scenarios on bldg_id, compute three delta measures.
insample_joined = (
    annual_default_hp
    .join(annual_seasonal_hp.drop("sb.electric_utility"), on=BLDG_ID, how="inner")
    .join(annual_tou_hp.drop("sb.electric_utility"),      on=BLDG_ID, how="inner")
    .join(
        metadata_insample.select([BLDG_ID, "weight", "postprocess_group.has_hp",
                                  HEATING_TYPE_COL, "in.sqft",
                                  "in.geometry_building_type_recs",
                                  "in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004",
                                  "in.vintage_acs", COOLING_COL]),
        on=BLDG_ID, how="left",
    )
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("bill_seasonal") - pl.col("bill_default")).alias("delta_seasonal_vs_default"),
        (pl.col("bill_tou")      - pl.col("bill_default")).alias("delta_tou_vs_default"),
        (pl.col("bill_tou")      - pl.col("bill_seasonal")).alias("delta_tou_vs_seasonal"),
    )
)

print(f"Joined in-sample HP buildings: {insample_joined.height:,}")
print("\nDelta summary (TOU − seasonal, in-sample HP households):")
s = insample_joined["delta_tou_vs_seasonal"]
print(
    f"  mean={s.mean():+.2f}  median={s.median():+.2f}  "
    f"min={s.min():+.2f}  max={s.max():+.2f}"
)
```

### 8d — Verify TOU outperforms seasonal in all utilities

TOU was fit to minimize cross-subsidization for in-sample HP households. We expect TOU to be cheaper
than seasonal for the majority of this group in every utility. The table below shows the weighted-mean
delta (TOU − seasonal), weighted-median delta, and the share of HP households that save under TOU vs.
seasonal, by utility. A negative mean delta and >50% saving confirm TOU performs better in-sample.

```{python}
#| label: verify-tou-better-insample

_util_verify = (
    insample_joined
    .group_by("sb.electric_utility")
    .agg(
        pl.col("weight").sum().alias("wtd_customers"),
        pl.len().alias("n_bldgs"),
        (
            (pl.col("delta_tou_vs_seasonal") * pl.col("weight")).sum()
            / pl.col("weight").sum()
        ).alias("wtd_mean_tou_vs_seasonal"),
        (
            pl.when(pl.col("delta_tou_vs_seasonal") < 0)
            .then(pl.col("weight"))
            .otherwise(0)
            .sum()
            / pl.col("weight").sum()
            * 100
        ).alias("pct_saving_wtd"),
        (
            (pl.col("delta_seasonal_vs_default") * pl.col("weight")).sum()
            / pl.col("weight").sum()
        ).alias("wtd_mean_seasonal_vs_default"),
        (
            (pl.col("delta_tou_vs_default") * pl.col("weight")).sum()
            / pl.col("weight").sum()
        ).alias("wtd_mean_tou_vs_default"),
    )
    .rename({"sb.electric_utility": "utility"})
    .join(
        pl.DataFrame({
            "utility": list(UTIL_NAMES.keys()),
            "utility_label": list(UTIL_NAMES.values()),
        }),
        on="utility", how="left",
    )
    .sort("wtd_mean_tou_vs_seasonal")
    .select([
        "utility_label", "n_bldgs", "wtd_customers",
        "wtd_mean_seasonal_vs_default",
        "wtd_mean_tou_vs_default",
        "wtd_mean_tou_vs_seasonal",
        "pct_saving_wtd",
    ])
)

# Sanity check: print which utilities have positive mean delta (TOU worse than seasonal)
_bad = _util_verify.filter(pl.col("wtd_mean_tou_vs_seasonal") > 0)
if _bad.height > 0:
    print(f"WARNING: {_bad.height} utilities where TOU is worse than seasonal on average:")
    print(_bad.select(["utility_label", "wtd_mean_tou_vs_seasonal"]))
else:
    print("✓ TOU outperforms seasonal (negative weighted mean delta) in all utilities.")

_bad_pct = _util_verify.filter(pl.col("pct_saving_wtd") < 50)
if _bad_pct.height > 0:
    print(f"WARNING: {_bad_pct.height} utilities where <50% of HP households save under TOU:")
    print(_bad_pct.select(["utility_label", "pct_saving_wtd"]))
else:
    print("✓ Majority of HP households save under TOU vs. seasonal in all utilities.")

(
    GT(_util_verify)
    .tab_header(
        title="TOU vs. Seasonal — in-sample HP households (upgrade 0)",
        subtitle=(
            "Runs 5+6 (seasonal) and 9+10 (TOU) vs. run 1+2 (default). "
            "Negative Δ = TOU cheaper than seasonal. Weighted statistics."
        ),
    )
    .cols_label(
        utility_label="Utility",
        n_bldgs="# Bldgs",
        wtd_customers="Wtd customers",
        wtd_mean_seasonal_vs_default="Seasonal − Default ($/yr)",
        wtd_mean_tou_vs_default="TOU − Default ($/yr)",
        wtd_mean_tou_vs_seasonal="TOU − Seasonal ($/yr)",
        pct_saving_wtd="% saving TOU vs seasonal (wtd)",
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns=["wtd_mean_seasonal_vs_default", "wtd_mean_tou_vs_default", "wtd_mean_tou_vs_seasonal"],
        decimals=2,
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=["pct_saving_wtd"], decimals=1)
    .fmt_integer(columns=["n_bldgs", "wtd_customers"])
    .tab_source_note(
        "In-sample HP households = buildings with postprocess_group.has_hp == True in upgrade 0. "
        "TOU − Seasonal < 0 means TOU is cheaper."
    )
)
```

### 8e — Delta distribution: TOU vs. seasonal (in-sample HP)

```{python}
#| label: fig-insample-delta-hist
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of annual bill delta (TOU − seasonal) for in-sample HP households (upgrade 0), weighted"

_BIN_W = 50  # smaller bins since in-sample deltas are tighter than out-of-sample

_bins_insample = (
    insample_joined.select("delta_tou_vs_seasonal", "weight")
    .with_columns(
        ((pl.col("delta_tou_vs_seasonal") / _BIN_W).floor() * _BIN_W + _BIN_W / 2).alias("bin_center"),
    )
    .group_by("bin_center")
    .agg(pl.col("weight").sum().alias("weight_sum"))
    .sort("bin_center")
)

_p01 = float(insample_joined["delta_tou_vs_seasonal"].quantile(0.01))
_p99 = float(insample_joined["delta_tou_vs_seasonal"].quantile(0.99))

(
    ggplot(_bins_insample, aes(x="bin_center", y="weight_sum"))
    + geom_col(width=_BIN_W * 0.85, fill=SB_COLORS["sky"], color="white", alpha=0.8)
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="red", linetype="dashed", size=1)
    + coord_cartesian(xlim=(_p01, _p99))
    + labs(
        title="TOU − seasonal bill delta: in-sample HP households (upgrade 0)",
        x="Annual bill change: TOU vs. seasonal ($/yr)",
        y="Weighted # of Homes",
    )
    + theme_switchbox()
    + theme(figure_size=(10.5, 4.5))
)
```

### 8f — Metadata characteristics of in-sample HP households

Who are the HP households in upgrade 0? These are the buildings whose loads the TOU windows were
optimized for. Understanding their characteristics clarifies which types of buildings the rate
was designed to serve.

```{python}
#| label: insample-metadata-setup

insample_hp_meta = metadata_insample.filter(pl.col("postprocess_group.has_hp")).with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("in.sqft") < 1000)
    .then(pl.lit("<1,000 sq ft"))
    .when(pl.col("in.sqft") < 2000)
    .then(pl.lit("1,000–2,000 sq ft"))
    .when(pl.col("in.sqft") < 3000)
    .then(pl.lit("2,000–3,000 sq ft"))
    .otherwise(pl.lit(">3,000 sq ft"))
    .alias("sqft_bin")
)

total_hp_weight = insample_hp_meta["weight"].sum()
print(f"In-sample HP buildings: {insample_hp_meta.height:,}")
print(f"Weighted HP customers:  {total_hp_weight:,.0f}")
```

The table below summarizes in-sample HP households across six dimensions, with weighted customer
counts and shares.

```{python}
#| label: insample-metadata-table

_META_DIMS: list[tuple[str, str, dict[str, str] | None]] = [
    ("sb.electric_utility",             "Utility",        UTIL_NAMES),
    ("in.geometry_building_type_recs",  "Building type",  None),
    ("in.ashrae_iecc_climate_zone_2004","Climate zone",   None),
    (COOLING_COL,                       "Cooling",        None),
    ("sqft_bin",                        "Floor area",     None),
    ("in.vintage_acs",                  "Vintage",        None),
]

_meta_rows: list[dict] = []
for col, dimension, label_map in _META_DIMS:
    for val in insample_hp_meta[col].drop_nulls().unique().to_list():
        grp = insample_hp_meta.filter(pl.col(col) == val)
        wtd_n = float(grp["weight"].sum())
        display_val = label_map.get(val, val) if label_map else val
        _meta_rows.append({
            "dimension": dimension,
            "segment": str(display_val),
            "n_bldgs": grp.height,
            "wtd_customers": int(round(wtd_n)),
            "pct_of_hp_customers": round(wtd_n / total_hp_weight * 100, 1),
        })

_meta_summary = (
    pl.DataFrame(_meta_rows)
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("dimension").cast(pl.Enum([d for _, d, _ in _META_DIMS])).alias("_dim_sort"),
    )
    .sort(["_dim_sort", "pct_of_hp_customers"], descending=[False, True])
    .drop("_dim_sort")
)

(
    GT(_meta_summary)
    .tab_header(
        title="In-sample HP household characteristics (upgrade 0)",
        subtitle=(
            "Buildings with postprocess_group.has_hp == True in upgrade 0. "
            "These are the households the TOU rate windows were fit to."
        ),
    )
    .cols_label(
        dimension="Dimension",
        segment="Segment",
        n_bldgs="# Bldgs (unweighted)",
        wtd_customers="Wtd customers",
        pct_of_hp_customers="% of HP customers",
    )
    .fmt_integer(columns=["n_bldgs", "wtd_customers"])
    .fmt_number(columns=["pct_of_hp_customers"], decimals=1)
    .tab_source_note(
        "% of HP customers = weighted share within the in-sample HP population. "
        "Weights rescaled to EIA residential customer count before HP filtering."
    )
)
```
